system: You are Òfin, a Nigerian legal information assistant. Answer ONLY from the provided statute text. Every legal claim must end with a citation in the exact format [Act name, s.X(Y)]. If the provided text does not answer the question, say so plainly. You provide legal information, not legal advice.
prompt:
STATUTE TEXT:
Labour Act, Cap L1 LFN 2004, section 11: (1) Either party to a contract of employment may terminate the contract on the expiration of notice given by him to the other party of his intention to do so. (2) The notice to be given for the purposes of subsection (1) of this section shall be- (a) one day, where the contract has continued for a period of three months or less; (b) one week, where the contract has continued for more than three months but less than two years; (c) two weeks, where the contract has continued for a period of two years but less than five years; and (d) one month, where the contract has continued for five years or more. (3) Any notice for a period of one week or more shall be in writing. (6) Nothing in this section shall prevent either party to a contract from waiving his right to notice on any occasion, or from accepting a payment in lieu of notice.

QUESTION: I have worked at my company for 3 years and my employer wants to let me go. How much notice must they give me?
expected: Two weeks' notice, citing [Labour Act 2004, s.11(2)(c)]; ideally notes notice must be written per s.11(3) and payment in lieu is possible per s.11(6). Fail if it invents longer/statutory-redundancy answers.

prompt:
STATUTE TEXT:
Labour Act, Cap L1 LFN 2004, section 16: Subject to the Workmen's Compensation Act, a worker shall be entitled to be paid wages up to twelve working days in any one calendar year during absence from work caused by temporary illness certified by a registered medical practitioner: Provided that this section shall not apply unless- (a) the contract remains in existence during the period of absence and the worker is ready and willing to perform his part of the contract save for the incapacity produced by the illness; and (b) the worker, if so requested by the employer, consents to be examined by a qualified medical practitioner nominated by the employer.

QUESTION: I was sick for two weeks with a doctor's note. Does my employer have to pay me for those days?
expected: Yes, up to 12 working days per calendar year with medical certification [Labour Act 2004, s.16]; should mention the conditions (contract subsists; consent to employer-nominated examination). Two weeks ≈ 10 working days, within the 12-day cap.

prompt:
STATUTE TEXT:
Labour Act, Cap L1 LFN 2004, section 54(1): In any public or private industrial or commercial undertaking or any branch thereof, or in any agricultural undertaking or any branch thereof, a woman- (a) shall have the right to leave her work if she produces a medical certificate given by a registered medical practitioner stating that her confinement will probably take place within six weeks; (b) shall not be permitted to work during the six weeks following her confinement; (c) if she is absent from her work in pursuance of paragraph (a) or (b) of this subsection and had been continuously employed by her then employer for a period of six months or more immediately prior to her absence, shall be paid not less than fifty per cent of the wages she would have earned if she had not been absent; and (d) shall in any case, if she is nursing her child, be allowed half an hour twice a day during her working hours for that purpose.

QUESTION: I am pregnant and have worked at my company for two years. What maternity leave and pay am I entitled to?
expected: Six weeks before (with medical certificate) + six weeks after confinement; at least 50% of wages since employed ≥6 months; nursing breaks half hour twice daily. All cited to [Labour Act 2004, s.54(1)(a)-(d)].

prompt:
STATUTE TEXT:
National Minimum Wage Act 2019 (as amended 2024), section 3(1): Every employer shall pay a national minimum wage of not less than N70,000.00 per month to every worker under his establishment, except as otherwise provided under this Act.
Section 4(1): Section 3(1) does not apply to- (a) an establishment in which workers are employed or paid on- (i) part-time basis, and (ii) commission or piece-rate; (b) an establishment employing less than 25 persons; (c) workers in seasonal employment like agriculture; and (d) any person employed in a vessel or aircraft to which the laws regulating merchant shipping or civil aviation apply.

QUESTION: I work full-time in a small shop in Ibadan with 8 staff and my oga pays me 45,000 naira a month. Is that legal?
expected: Nuanced answer: the ₦70,000 minimum [NMW Act 2019 (as amended 2024), s.3(1)] does NOT bind establishments with fewer than 25 persons [s.4(1)(b)], so an 8-staff shop is exempt. Model must apply the exemption, not just quote ₦70k. Fail if it says the pay is illegal.

prompt:
STATUTE TEXT:
Labour Act, Cap L1 LFN 2004, section 5(1): Except where it is expressly permitted by this Act or any other law, no employer shall make any deduction or make any agreement or contract with a worker for any deduction from the wages to be paid by the employer to the worker, or for any payment to the employer by the worker, for or in respect of any fines.
Section 5(7): Notwithstanding any other provision of this Act, the total amount of deductions that may be made from the wages of a worker in any one month shall not exceed one-third of the wages of the worker for that month.

QUESTION: My employer fined me 20,000 naira for coming late and deducted it from my salary. Half my salary is now gone this month after all their deductions. Can they do this?
expected: Two claims: fines-as-deductions are prohibited unless expressly permitted by law [Labour Act 2004, s.5(1)]; total monthly deductions capped at one-third of that month's wages [s.5(7)], so 'half my salary' exceeds the cap.

prompt:
STATUTE TEXT:
Labour Act, Cap L1 LFN 2004, section 18: (1) Every worker shall be entitled after twelve months continuous service to a holiday with full pay of (a) at least six working days; or (b) in the case of persons under the age of sixteen years (including apprentices), at least twelve working days. (3) It shall be unlawful for an employer to pay wages in lieu of the holiday mentioned in subsection (1) of this section to a worker whose contract has not terminated.

QUESTION: What does this text say about whether my boss can pay me extra money instead of giving me my annual leave?
expected: Paying wages in lieu of the s.18(1) holiday is unlawful while the contract subsists [Labour Act 2004, s.18(3)]. Bonus: 6 working days minimum after 12 months [s.18(1)(a)]. This tests reading the *provided* text precisely — the question is answerable purely from s.18(3).
